sentences.text: --telling us about how the prisoners were being transported on a train towards Theresien.
There we got stuck on this train station.
And there many of my fellow prisoners died.
It was raining, so as it was difficult to sit on the floor of the wagons.
They were full of water, so we sat on our poor dead fellow prisoners, who didn't mind at that point.
At that point, they let us bury some of our dead comrades.
And there was a fight between the SS and the railway employees, who insisted that they will give us our-- their ration, their food.
And that was the first time after two days when we got some food.
Water was-- that wasn't bad for it was raining.
But the food, that was tough.
So we got something.
And we are seeing the tracks-- the trains with the wounded German soldiers trekking back towards Germany.
And we were stuck for two days standing there.
And suddenly, on the night between 8 and 9 of May, we didn't realize that this-- 7 and 8 of May, sorry-- that the war came to an end.
They hitched an engine to us, and they dragged us to something called Litomarice, L-I-T-O-M-A-R-I-C-E, which was a-- we were stopped there on a train station.
And from there, it was still, blackout.
Everything was in the darkness.
And in the distance we have seen a lit huge space, a city.
We knew it's Theresien.
Theresien was lit.
Something very unusual.
And we got the order to get out of the wagons.
No [INAUDIBLE].
No beating.
And the-- the whip was a short guy who was standing on a box and was telling us that if we marched orderly-- that it's night.

And if we drag on the small wagons which we'll get all our dead comrades, and if we help anybody who can't walk, that we will march in front of Theresien, and they will transfer us to the International Red Cross.
We didn't capiche so quickly.
But we have seen one thing.
We have seen this lit Theresien.
We have seen that we are being handled a little bit differently.
And then [INAUDIBLE], the whip, says, and remember that whatever we ever did was an order.
We never did anything of our own initiative.
And should you be ever asked, you will answer that way.
And we started to march.
And as we were, we are really marched.
Yeah?
We came to the River Elbe, which is one of the main rivers there ending in Hamburg.
And as we were crossing it, on both sides were Russian soldiers.
They were Ukrainian soldiers of the Vlasov's Army, V-L-A-S-O-V.
Was a general who turned against the Russians and was fighting on the side of the Germans.
And at that point, they were turning against the Germans.
But they let us and the SS men walk through.
And we walked about another couple of kilometers.
And then they ordered, stop.
And they came into formation by three, and they turned around and marched away.
And as we were, we jumped into the ditches which were on the side of the road, and we were lying there expecting that they will spray us with the machine guns.
And nothing like that ever happened.
And then after a few minutes, we have seen two buses coming close.
And they were empty.
They were fully lit.
On one was written Czech Red Cross.
And on the other was written Croix-Rouge International.

And the drivers came out and he says, oh, my god, you look-- but try to walk into Theresien, for if we take you, you will have to stay in the quarantine camp, and that may take you a few weeks to be free.
Try to walk.
So we walked.
Oh, we just couldn't-- couldn't get it, you know, what is happening.
That it happened.
Oh, they have seen these haggard there.
[INAUDIBLE] for.
We were probably not the only ones who came, I mean, there on trains and similar.
They were gathering there prisoners already for-- for a few days.
They had ordered a quarantine camp, and there was really-- the International Red Cross was in Theresien.
And so we marched.
And then we came to this barrier, and there was the casino, which I mentioned, with the gendarmes.
And there is the gendarmes stand-- standing at the-- kind of this thing or this barrier.
The rod which is put across the road.
And he says to us, in German, halt!
And in German, sit down and wait.
And my friend, George Wassermann, is-- we are sitting there quite stunned, realizing that there is no SS.
There is only the gendarmes.
And we look at the building, and on the building there are two flags hanging.
And there is-- it's lit.
So it's a bad lighting, but still light.
And I see a white flag with a red cross, and next to it is a white, red, with a blue triangle.
A flag of former Czechoslovakia, which was-- I mean, it was automatic arrest and automatic concentration camp to show it.
And I looked at the gendarme, and on his hat-- cap, on his hood, he has got a little tricolor with the Czechoslovak flag.
And I go to him and I say, in Czech, what's the meaning of this?
He says, Christ!
You speak Czech?
I says-- using a bad word-- we all speak Czech.
We all came from this town two years ago, simply.
And we are just returning.
He says, oh, mother of God.
And runs into the casino.
And they put the floodlight on us, and out runs an officer, I mean, who is just closing his uniform.
And steps on the barrier, and he says the first word is he says, brethren, using an ancient word for brothers, I welcome you back as the citizens of free Czechoslovakia.
And we had been 195 at that point.
And 15 of us could walk.
The rest they had to help to the gathering station, for they simply collapsed.
They were out.
Most of them-- I mean, if you add in over 200, about five died still in Theresien of exhaustion.
And a little funny vignette, as we were walking with George Wassermann through the street toward the place where they send us.
This also a small military barracks.
He says, there you will get food and everything.
So we walk through the street, and he says, here in this house lived my mother.
She was a famous Czech coutier, haute coutier, dressmaker par premium.
He says, oh, she's naturally somewhere in the Auschwitz ovens.
I said, don't be silly.
Let's try.
We walk in.
There is the room where she was supposed to live.
There is light on, and there is a lady, old lady-- probably younger than I am now-- bent over reading.
Mrs.
Wassermann.
And we make a little noise so she turns around.
She looks, looks, looks.
And says, George?
George, it's really you?
He was the only son.
It's really you?
Look how you look so dirty.
Go and wash, was her first reaction.
[LAUGHS]
But we continued, for we had to go there.
Anyhow, we got food, and we got washed.
And the nurses simply were absolutely very sad to see this.
And they did absolutely everything.
They fed us with risotto rice, with a little bit of [INAUDIBLE] in it.
And we drank milk.
And we were put on straw mats, which were covered by white sheets and white [INAUDIBLE] white pillow.
And they said, just sleep sleep the night through.
And I woke, woke up George.
It was about 5:00 in the morning, and we realized we are hungry.
We said to the nurse, how about some food?
She says, come.
I'll take you to the kitchen.
So the cook did some eggs for us.
He says, don't put any fat on it, nothing.
I mean, the guys cannot stand it.
But anyhow, we got bread and eggs.
And there appeared a guy on the scene.
He says, the bus, our bus for Prague, will be leaving in about-- we are leaving in about half an hour.
And we could hear tremendous noise.
The Russians were pulling towards Prague, which was already free.
But they were liberating Prague.
And we said, we are going with you.
He says, those-- he says, no.
We are going with you.
He says, sure, but you need some documents.

So there was, I think, the military commander of this who made-- made some kind of documents, written, but put the necessary steps on it that our name is so and so, and we have been liberated that and that day from this and that camp.
And with that, we entered the bus.
It was early in the morning.
The Russians simply-- we had the Russian inscription on the-- and the Czech inscription that the martyrs of Theresien are welcoming you, Prague.
Somebody made these two banners.
And they let us through while the Germans-- they were dragging the German prisoners up.
So between the tanks and between the army, we simply wheeled our way.
We got to the City of Melnik, where we were welcomed.
Obviously somebody must have had some connection.
We were welcomed there by a welcoming committee.
They were throwing us cigarettes and bread and everything.
And for the first time-- and the last time, probably-- in the history of the Czech [INAUDIBLE] we were hearing, long live the Jews!
[CZECH].
And so we went to Prague.
And suddenly the bus was going through a city where the street cars were running.
And at one point, the bus driver said, whoever lives here, or close here, so you can step out.
Otherwise, we are going to our final destination is-- actually, it was the medical-- the-- the-- the complex of the-- the Czech College of Physicians and Surgeons.
And that's where I was thrown out in Prague.
And we entered a street car.
We didn't have the tickets.
The conductor found out that we have got numbers on the thing.
He stopped the street car.
People were giving us money, giving us addresses, giving us cigarettes, getting hugs.
And we got to George's apartment, which was empty.
And we are free.
When the-- when the-- when the man told us 'you are citizens of free Czechoslovakia.'
Yeah.
Uh-huh.
I went the same day.
I went to this address where I found her letter.
These were her cousins, or second-- second round cousins.
And I knew that she's in Hamburg.
She was in Hamburg at that point in time.
So first I went there.
I found out that there is a special kind of department of citizenship, which gathers the lists of liberated prisoners from all over the-- all over Europe.
Whether there is anything from Hamburg, which it wasn't.
She wasn't-- there was a list from the Hamburg-- from Hamburg, but she was not on the list.

Then I got myself an apartment, according to the wishes which we had with Eva that we will look for something like my offices upstairs-- one big room, a modern bathroom, in a modern building, with elevator, with modern kitchen, with everything.
And I got it allotted from the Czech authorities.
It was seized from some Germans.
No.
I didn't [INAUDIBLE].
I returned, but somebody was living there, and it would be very difficult to dislodge them.
So they gave me a replacement apartment.
And they gave a replacement apartment to my mother when she returned.
And I was going to this address simply looking for Eva regularly, every day.

And about 10 days later, I found out that there were two guys there who was-- who are saying that they were going to Hamburg to bring the list of the liberated prisoners plus one very kind of prominent Czech writer who was liberated in Kiel, K-I-E-L, in a camp.
And so I said, I'm going with you.
He says, hold it, hold it, hold it.
We need an interpreter.
I said, you just found one.
Yeah.
And I proclaimed myself for an interpreter.
They slept over in my place, and then we entered in a truck, which was a Chevrolet 1922, which did not make the mountains quite.
But we got through the Russians, and we got it repaired.
And we simply got ourself to the vicinity of the Belsen, where I knew that 300 of our guys landed in Belsen.
And with the help of a Swiss officer, we got into the camp, which was out of limits for there was really typhoid and everything.
We didn't know that we are not in Belsen, that Belsen was burned, but that we are in Bergen, which were SS military-- SS [GERMAN], hospital barracks.
And everybody was located there.
There was a liaison officer, a Czech liaison officer.
I found out-- there were little squares, and there was a Czech flag.
I went to the liaison officer-- who was living there in Toronto here for quite a while-- and he gave me the list of the liberated prisoners.
There were dozens of names which I knew, but Eva wasn't on it.
And meanwhile, one of the girls spotted me, so I was surrounded by dozens and dozens of kids who were hanging on me and was asking, my husband?
My brother?
My beloved one?
God knows what I said.
Kids, my memory is limited.
Please, let's sit down and ask one after the other.
I will try.
And if I knew that the person is alive, so it was a great jubilation.
If I knew that the person died, I kept it for myself.
And after about 15, 20 minutes, somebody says, did you see Eva at all?
I said, Eva?
She's alive?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm sorry.
[SOBBING] I'm sorry.
[SOBBING] [SIGHS]
I found out that she's in a hospital, that she's there with typhoid and typhus.
She had a combination of both.
And that she's working there as an interpreter.
That she's weak, but she certainly will be able to [SIGHS] see me.

And one of her friends, Ruth, who lives in Israel and who was our best friend and became a great journalist and was a great friend of ours, who was kind of a-- had twins during the experiences in the camp.
So she ran for her.
And she told her, oh, hurry, hurry.
We have made an arrangement, when there is a possibility, that we go back to Prague.
So you simply have yet to register, so she escaped from the hospital under the wire-- barbed wire.
And she was running.
So the kids were yelling at her, congratulations.
She didn't capiche, didn't understand what was going on.
And then the door opens, and she sees me, whereupon she faints flat.
And we are trying to revive her, so she asked about her father who, meanwhile, returned.
So she faints again.
And simply that's how I found her.
She dieted quite well for-- her weight was 37 kilo.

And I had to go with the guys anyhow to Kiel, and on the way back we picked up Eva, plus some friends, and we drove with a-- a huge seized German car, which the two guys stole there from a military depot.
And every military police stopped us, but on the numbers let us go.
And we returned to Prague.
It was the beginning of June, 1945.
We left Czechoslovakia on the 30th of November, 1948, when it became Communist.
It became Communist in the spring.
We got ourself legally out.
We waited for the Canadian visa in Switzerland, and we came.
We landed in Fort Erie on the 6th of March, 1949.
Today we've got three children.
I start with the oldest, Dan, who is married, who's got three children.
Our oldest is 19.
He's at the university, the second year.
And there is one, 17, Paul, who was here last week visiting with us.
And then we have got Claire, who is 12 years old.
Grandchildren.
And then I have a son, Peter, who lives in Boston.
Daniel lives in Nashville.
And Peter lives in Boston, and he's got one child, Daniella.
And then our youngest, Michelle, calls herself Misha, M-I-S-H-A.
And she's got two children.
One of them is Noah, who is-- Daniella is five plus and Noah is four plus.
And little Sasha, who will be two years old.
They all are healthy.
[KNOCKING] And I hope that they will have the patience to listen to me.
Yes.
Actually, one of my grandsons, Paul, was the instigator of this.
He wrote to the Holocaust Museum, and then he registered me with the Spielberg Foundation.
Yeah.
I was three months short finishing medicine in Prague.
I left, for we know that we will be arrested.
And in Switzerland, I could have continued, but I had a visa to Canada.
We had to transit through the States.
I could have stayed probably in the States, but we went to Canada.
And I tried to get back into medicine.
It took me two and a half years.
I had 189 negative responses, for at that point the veterans were entering medical schools.
And meanwhile, I started to study at the Universite de Montreal, where I got from them a master's degree there.
It recognized my bachelor on account of the 13 grades.
And a master degree in Slavic literature.
And that enabled me then to get to Queens, a little bit.
But I got to Queens, to medical school, by a fluke.
And I finished medicine.
I was in grave debt, so I decided after two years of residency to go into general practice so that I pay my debt back.
And meanwhile, I-- four years later-- got my mother into Canada, who came in and had a full stroke in the plane.
And she stayed in the hospital for an enormous quantity of time, and our finances were drained so I stayed in general practice.
I became very, very successful at that point.
And just out of curiosity, I wanted to continue to study.
So in 1965, after taking many courses, some of them together with Eva, I get the PhD in Literature from the Universite de Montreal.
I continued with my practice.
In 1969, I had decided that it's the highest time I switched to something else.
I took training in psychoanalysis and additional training in psychiatric training.
And in 1975, I got qualified.
And I started doing solely psychotherapy, with a certain exception for all my friends who came with me-- who went with me in the camp.
They did not recognize that I am doing psychiatry for them.
I'm still simply a practitioner, which is quite good.
And that's how I came to Canada.
Just, [INAUDIBLE], probably.
Be prepared.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I believe simply that maybe humanity learned something, but in very small doses.
You know, the cruelty of man to man has no end, and the kind of division between people is being kept artificially by so many means and ways.
That the-- they have to have their eyes and ears open and be well-trained and well-prepared.
And I hope that they inherited some, maybe, of the intuitiveness I might have inherited from my forefathers.
Thank you.
